network.overall_strategy: This network centers on the best-supported prebiotic route to isoleucine: volcanic/spark-discharge generation of HCN and the key branched aldehyde 2-methylbutanal, followed by Strecker aminonitrile formation and hydrolysis in surface aqueous settings. To satisfy environmental interconnection while remaining literature-guided, hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a feeder network that supplies simple carbon substrates such as formate, acetate, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and glyoxylate, but not direct isoleucine synthesis, consistent with reports that hydrothermal simulations have not demonstrated isoleucine formation. Additional target-reaching routes are included only where the literature explicitly supports the chemistry in general but leaves the specific isoleucine precursor availability unresolved: Bucherer-Bergs and reductive amination/photocatalytic amination of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid are therefore marked as speculative endpoint branches dependent on a difficult keto-acid precursor. The network is organized around hub molecules HCN, 2-methylbutanal, pyruvate, glyoxylate, and 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid, with ten distinct pathways converging on isoleucine or its immediate aminonitrile/keto-acid precursors.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of CO2 to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, anoxic, H2-rich

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 activation followed by stepwise hydrogenation to surface-bound formyl/formate species.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S mineral catalysis of CO2 reduction to formate is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent-analog chemistry, with greigite and mackinawite acting as prebiotic [Fe-S] analogs; included here as feeder chemistry rather than a direct isoleucine-forming step, in line with the conclusion that hydrothermal systems support simple carbon fixation but have not demonstrated isoleucine synthesis (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Hall, 2006; Martin & Russell, 2007; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: FeS/Fe0-mediated carbon fixation to acetate

conditions.other: anoxic vent pore setting with reducing power from H2

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond formation from one-carbon species on iron-sulfur/iron surfaces gives simple C2 carboxylates.
reactions.reasoning: This step represents conservative hydrothermal feeder chemistry to small organics rather than a claim of direct isoleucine synthesis. It is aligned with FeS/NiS and iron-driven carbon-fixation analogs from hydrothermal literature and is used here only to connect vent carbon fixation to later surface chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Hall, 2006; Martin & Russell, 2007; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Magnetite-catalyzed Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: subseafloor or vent-associated reducing conditions; product expected as mixture component

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type surface reduction of carbon oxides generates mixed low-molecular-weight oxygenates including aldehydes.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report identifies Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on metal surfaces as one plausible source of the aldehyde precursor family needed for Strecker chemistry, but not as a clean, selective route; accordingly acetaldehyde is treated as a mixed-product feeder intermediate and not overstated (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis on magnetite/greigite

conditions.other: anoxic alkaline hydrothermal setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond forming chemistry yields alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate in protometabolic networks.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is included as a central protometabolic hub supported by prebiotic metabolism literature and as a plausible hydrothermal product, but this does not imply hydrothermal synthesis of isoleucine itself; the deep research explicitly notes hydrothermal systems are more relevant to keto-acid feeder chemistry than to direct isoleucine production (Springsteen et al., 2018; Ter-Ovanessian et al., 2025; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Oxidative feeder formation of glyoxylate from formate/carboxylate pools

conditions.other: protometabolic vent network; included as a network connection to glyoxylate chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Fe-mediated carbonyl chemistry in protometabolic mixtures provides access to C2 oxoacids such as glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a well-established central prebiotic metabolite in the literature summary and is necessary to connect pyruvate-centered protometabolism to alpha-ketobutyrate ideas; this exact feeder step is network-completion chemistry and is therefore more speculative than the Strecker core, but is constrained to simple protometabolic oxoacid generation rather than unsupported isoleucine synthesis (Springsteen et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2020; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Volcanic spark discharge generation of HCN

conditions.other: electrical discharge in reducing volcanic atmosphere; H2S-enhanced radical chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge fragments reduced gases to radicals and nitriles; HCN accumulates and dissolves into aqueous surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: HCN production in Miller-Urey-type discharge systems is a foundational result for Strecker chemistry, and Parker et al. identified H2S-containing volcanic discharge conditions as particularly productive for branched amino acid synthesis. Mineral agents are included because the schema requires them for surface reactions, but the main energy source is electrical discharge (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Volcanic spark discharge generation of 2-methylbutanal

conditions.other: electrical discharge; radical recombination promoted by H2S-derived sulfur radicals

reactions.mechanism: Discharge-generated alkyl radicals undergo recombination and oxidation to branched aldehydes, including the crucial C5 precursor 2-methylbutanal.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies generation of 2-methylbutanal as the fundamental bottleneck and specifically notes radical chemistry in spark discharge, especially under H2S-containing volcanic conditions, as the strongest experimental context for access to isoleucine precursors. This is the literature-supported core precursor-forming step (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Crossed-aldol access to 2-methylbutanal from acetaldehyde and propanal

conditions.other: evaporitic pool wet-dry concentration; pathway remains chemically plausible but less direct than discharge radical route

reactions.mechanism: Surface-promoted carbonyl condensation among small aldehydes gives a branched C5 aldehyde after dehydration/reduction sequence in mixed prebiotic pools.
reactions.reasoning: The research report explicitly lists crossed aldol condensation of acetaldehyde with propanal as one plausible route to 2-methylbutanal, but not as strongly demonstrated as spark discharge chemistry. It is therefore included as a secondary, literature-mentioned route to the same critical hub and should be regarded as plausible but less established (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; Ross & Deamer, 2016).

reactions.name: Aqueous Strecker synthesis of isoleucine aminonitrile from 2-methylbutanal

conditions.other: imine formation followed by cyanide addition in aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by nucleophilic HCN addition to give the alpha-aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the dominant, best-supported route for prebiotic isoleucine formation. Miller identified Strecker chemistry as the principal mechanism in spark discharge products, and the specific isoleucine route from 2-methylbutanal, NH3, and HCN is directly described in the deep research summary (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Eckhardt et al., 2022; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of isoleucine aminonitrile to racemic isoleucine

conditions.other: gives racemic isoleucine plus alloisoleucine mixture under prebiotic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis through amide/carboxylate stages converts the aminonitrile into the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the Strecker aminonitrile is the final stage of the canonical prebiotic isoleucine route. The literature emphasizes that this process yields racemic material and does not solve the dual-chirality problem, but it remains the strongest experimentally grounded route to isoleucine (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Bada & Schroeder, 1975).

reactions.name: Pyruvate-glyoxylate route to alpha-ketobutyrate

conditions.other: proto-metabolic network; nonenzymatic analogue inferred from underground biosynthetic logic

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl coupling and rearrangement within alpha-keto acid/glyoxylate networks generates higher oxoacids including alpha-ketobutyrate-like products.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research specifically highlights alpha-ketobutyrate as a biologically central branch-point and notes underground biosynthetic routes from glyoxylate + pyruvate as prebiotically significant because both are central prebiotic metabolites. This specific prebiotic conversion remains unresolved and is therefore included as a speculative connector, not as an experimentally established isoleucine synthesis step (Kim et al., 2020; Springsteen et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Assembly of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid from alpha-ketobutyrate and pyruvate equivalents

conditions.other: protometabolic aldol/ketol chemistry; key unresolved bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic carbon-carbon bond formation extends the alpha-ketobutyrate skeleton to the branched C6 alpha-keto acid corresponding to isoleucine's direct keto precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly identifies alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate as the penultimate biological intermediate and notes that its prebiotic formation might be accessible through nonenzymatic aldol chemistry, but that this has not been demonstrated. It is therefore a necessary but clearly speculative bottleneck node in metabolism-first branches (Ter-Ovanessian et al., 2025; deep research conclusion).

reactions.name: FeS-mediated reductive amination of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid to isoleucine

conditions.other: reductive amination analogue; included outside Hydrothermal class to avoid claiming vent de novo isoleucine synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia addition to the alpha-keto acid followed by mineral-mediated reduction yields the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-oxo acids to amino acids is experimentally demonstrated with FeS/Fe(OH)2 systems, but the deep research stresses that isoleucine production by this route depends entirely on the prior availability of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid and that hydrothermal vent simulations have not produced isoleucine. Accordingly this is an explicitly conditional, speculative endpoint branch (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wachtershauser, 2003; Cleaves et al., 2009).

reactions.name: ZnS-UV photocatalytic amination of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid to isoleucine

conditions.other: shallow illuminated settings; reversible amination chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited ZnS generates electrons and holes that drive reversible reductive amination of alpha-keto acids.
reactions.reasoning: The failed prior attempt incorrectly emphasized TiO2 for this chemistry; the literature specifically identifies ZnS as the experimentally supported photocatalyst for reversible amination of alpha-keto acids. As with rxn_013, applicability to isoleucine is conditional on availability of the difficult branched alpha-keto precursor (Wang et al., 2012; deep research conclusion).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid to isoleucine hydantoin

conditions.other: cyanide/ammonia-mediated hydantoin formation from alpha-keto acids

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid reacts with cyanide and ammonia to give a hydantoin intermediate via Bucherer-Bergs chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs as a bridge between Strecker-type prebiotic chemistry and biologically relevant alpha-keto acid substrates. The deep research states the chemistry is general but unresolved for isoleucine because precursor availability is the main problem, so this branch is included as a conditional but literature-grounded alternative endpoint route (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of isoleucine hydantoin to isoleucine

conditions.other: post-Bucherer-Bergs ring opening and hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening and hydrolysis furnish the amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the terminal step of the Bucherer-Bergs route. Included because the literature identifies Bucherer-Bergs as a demonstrated prebiotic amino-acid-forming process generalizable to alpha-keto acids, while acknowledging unresolved access to the required branched precursor for isoleucine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Meteorite parent-body Strecker synthesis to isoleucine aminonitrile precursor pool

conditions.temperature: low to moderate parent-body aqueous alteration conditions
conditions.other: extraterrestrial aqueous chemistry preserved in carbonaceous chondrites

reactions.mechanism: Parent-body aqueous Strecker chemistry from branched aldehydes forms isoleucine aminonitrile and then amino acid upon hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: The research summary explicitly states meteoritic/parent-body Strecker chemistry is one of the three experimentally supported contexts for prebiotic isoleucine, with isotopic evidence showing the existence of the branched aldehyde precursor in meteorite parent bodies. This reaction node captures that independent source to the same aminonitrile hub (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; Cronin et al., 1981; Glavin et al., 2021).

reactions.name: UV/EUV photolysis of PAH-containing ices yielding isoleucine

conditions.temperature: cryogenic ice photolysis followed by warming
conditions.other: PAH-rich ice analog chemistry as extraterrestrial feedstock deposited to surface

reactions.mechanism: Energetic photolysis of aromatic ice mixtures generates complex organics that, upon warm-up/hydrolysis, include amino acids such as isoleucine.
reactions.reasoning: Chen et al. reported isoleucine among amino acids produced by UV/EUV photolysis of naphthalene + H2O + NH3 ices. This is an experimentally positive but non-terrestrial-assembly route; in the network it functions as an independent source of the target entering surface reservoirs, not as a contradiction to the dominant Strecker pipeline (Chen et al., 2008; Bernstein et al., 2002; Munoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Surface concentration and preservation of isoleucine in wet-dry pools

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling, urea-rich or mineral interfaces, concentration up to ~1400-fold

reactions.mechanism: No net chemical transformation; concentration by evaporation, adsorption, and cycling increases local availability for further prebiotic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Although not a synthetic transformation to a new molecule, concentration is essential for a trace amino acid such as isoleucine. The literature emphasizes wet-dry cycling and pool concentration as critical for preserving and accumulating scarce products from discharge or exogenous input (Ross & Deamer, 2016; Ferris, 2005; Zhang et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminobutyric acid as a branch-comparison route

conditions.other: canonical Strecker chemistry from propanal

reactions.mechanism: Propanal undergoes imine formation and cyanide addition followed by hydrolysis to give alpha-aminobutyric acid.
reactions.reasoning: The research highlights alpha-aminobutyric acid as an easier 4-carbon analogue that is readily formed in Miller experiments and meteorites, illustrating the carbon-skeleton bottleneck specific to isoleucine. This branch is included to show network convergence around HCN and aldehyde chemistry and to contextualize why isoleucine is rare (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; deep research summary).

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S spark-discharge Strecker route
pathways.description: Best-supported terrestrial route: H2S-containing spark discharge generates both HCN and branched aldehyde precursor 2-methylbutanal, which undergoes Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Classic reducing-atmosphere spark-discharge Strecker route
pathways.description: Variant of the discharge route supported by Ring et al. detection of isoleucine in CH4/N2/NH3/H2O spark mixtures; converges on the same Strecker core.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation feeding surface aldol-Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal production of acetaldehyde feeds surface carbonyl chemistry, where crossed-aldol access to 2-methylbutanal converges with HCN-mediated Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Meteorite parent-body Strecker route
pathways.description: Extraterrestrial branched aldehydes and HCN/NH3 aqueous chemistry produce the same aminonitrile intermediate later delivered to or mirrored on Earth.

pathways.name: PAH-ice UV photolysis route
pathways.description: Independent experimentally positive route in which UV/EUV photolysis of PAH-containing ices yields isoleucine directly after warm-up/hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Protometabolic keto-acid route with FeS reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and glyoxylate feed a speculative alpha-ketobutyrate-to-alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate branch, followed by FeS-mediated reductive amination to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Protometabolic keto-acid route with ZnS photocatalytic amination
pathways.description: Same speculative branched keto-acid assembly as P6, but the final amino-acid-forming step uses the literature-supported ZnS UV amination chemistry rather than FeS reductive amination.

pathways.name: Protometabolic Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Branched alpha-keto acid formed in a speculative protometabolic network is converted by Bucherer-Bergs chemistry through a hydantoin intermediate to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface mixed feeder route to Strecker isoleucine
pathways.description: Vent chemistry supplies simple carbon pools, while surface discharge produces HCN and branched aldehyde; environmental crossover culminates in standard Strecker hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Concentrated surface-pool preservation pathway
pathways.description: Once generated by discharge Strecker or exogenous input, isoleucine is concentrated and preserved in wet-dry pools to become available for later prebiotic chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway in the network and aligns closely with the literature. H2S-containing spark discharge is specifically supported for isoleucine production, and the Strecker-to-hydrolysis sequence is the best-established prebiotic route. Minor uncertainty remains because direct prebiotic availability of 2-methylbutanal is low-yield, but the pathway is still well supported.
suggestions: Clarify that the product of spark chemistry is racemic isoleucine plus alloisoleucine after hydrolysis.
Add explicit aqueous phase separation between gas-phase discharge generation and solution-phase Strecker chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Strecker core is strong, but the literature indicates isoleucine is especially associated with the H2S-containing discharge experiment rather than generic reducing-atmosphere discharge. This route is plausible as a variant, but it is less directly supported than the volcanic sulfur-rich version.
suggestions: Distinguish CH4/N2/NH3/H2O and H2S-rich discharge conditions explicitly.
Reduce confidence in branched aldehyde generation without sulfur radical chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the intended hydrothermal-to-surface interconnection, but the chemistry feeding 2-methylbutanal is weak. Acetaldehyde formation hydrothermally is plausible, yet the crossed-aldol route is underdefined because propanal is not well established in the network and aldol/deoxygenation to the stated aldehyde is not chemically clean as written.
suggestions: Introduce an explicit propanal-forming reaction supported by discharge or FTT chemistry.
Split the aldol route into beta-hydroxy aldehyde formation plus dehydration/reduction steps.
Avoid implying direct clean conversion from acetaldehyde plus propanal to 2-methylbutanal in one step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Meteorite parent-body Strecker chemistry is well aligned with the literature and isotopic evidence supports the aldehyde precursor existing off-world. The main limitation is that this is not strongly integrated with the terrestrial hydrothermal-surface flow, so it functions more as an auxiliary source than a core Earth-surface pathway.
suggestions: Clarify transfer from parent-body synthesis to terrestrial aqueous hydrolysis after delivery.
Note meteoritic support is strongest for precursor existence and amino acid occurrence, not necessarily complete in situ hydrolysis on parent bodies.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is literature-supported by PAH-containing ice photolysis experiments, but it sits somewhat outside the rest of the network and lacks explicit precursor detail. It reaches the target, yet contributes little to the integrated terrestrial synthesis story.
suggestions: Specify the PAH-containing ice substrate set rather than only NH3 and H2O.
Treat this as an exogenous delivery or parallel source pathway rather than a central terrestrial route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The endpoint reductive amination step has some literature support in general for alpha-keto acids, but the upstream construction of alpha-ketobutyrate and especially 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid is highly speculative here. The network overstates biochemical continuity where the literature explicitly identifies this as unresolved.
suggestions: Mark the whole keto-acid route as speculative with lower confidence.
Add explicit justification or literature citation for glyoxylate-to-ketobutyrate conversion under prebiotic conditions.
Do not present alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate formation as established.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: ZnS photocatalytic amination of alpha-keto acids is supported in general, so the final step is somewhat plausible. However, as in P6, the upstream supply of the required branched keto acid is the core unresolved problem, making the pathway weak overall.
suggestions: Explicitly state that only the amination chemistry is experimentally grounded, not the precursor synthesis.
Provide a more plausible surface photochemical context if ZnS is retained.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bucherer-Bergs via hydantoin is a reasonable generalized transformation for alpha-keto acids, and the hydantoin hydrolysis step is acceptable. But the same major weakness persists: the network does not convincingly establish prebiotic access to the required branched keto acid precursor.
suggestions: Retain as a speculative branch only.
Clarify that generality from pyruvate does not equal demonstrated access for the isoleucine precursor.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway does a good job of showing environmental flow from hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface synthesis, but the key carbon-skeleton-forming step remains the weakest point. It is a useful network connector rather than a literature-best route to isoleucine.
suggestions: Strengthen the hydrothermal feeder chemistry with only well-supported small-molecule outputs.
Move 2-methylbutanal generation back to spark/radical chemistry as the dominant source.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a strong extension of the main Strecker pathway into plausible surface concentration and preservation. The preservation node is not really a chemical transformation and is somewhat awkward as a reaction, but the environmental logic is sound.
suggestions: Represent preservation/concentration as a process annotation rather than a self-loop reaction.
If retained, specify wet-dry concentration, mineral adsorption, or eutectic concentration more explicitly.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically rich and clearly centered on the literature-supported isoleucine solution space: spark-discharge generation of HCN and branched aldehydes followed by Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis. It succeeds in producing the target by multiple routes, includes strong hubs, and shows reasonable hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical interconnection. The strongest part is the volcanic H2S spark-discharge Strecker branch, which is highly consistent with Ring et al. 1972 and Parker et al. 2011. The meteorite and PAH-ice branches are also literature-grounded auxiliary sources. However, the overall network falls below approval because several reactions are chemically weak or overcommitted relative to the literature. The largest problem is the speculative keto-acid branch: glyoxylate/pyruvate to alpha-ketobutyrate and especially to 3-methyl-2-oxopentanoic acid is not established prebiotically, yet it is used to support three target-reaching pathways. The crossed-aldol route to 2-methylbutanal is also underdefined and missing a convincing source for propanal. Hydrothermal chemistry is appropriately conservative in not claiming vent synthesis of isoleucine directly, but some feeder reactions such as glyoxylate formation and direct pyruvate synthesis are only moderately supported and need tighter justification. There are also schema-level issues: some required mineral specificity and literature citations are absent from the evaluation input, one molecule name is mislabeled as 2-methylbutyraldehyde instead of 2-methylbutanal, Biochemical reactions use mineral-style catalysts outside the stated requirement framework, and the self-loop preservation reaction is not a true reaction. Overall, this is a good but not excellent network: strong core route, weaker speculative branches, and incomplete literature-grounding at the reaction level.
